title: The Neuroscience of Crying During Psychedelic Sessions and Why It Heals
body:
The Neuroscience of Crying During Psychedelic Sessions and Why It Heals

1. Opening Hook: Tears in the Quiet

I remember the first time I cried during a psychedelic session. It wasn’t the sobbing, dramatic kind you see in movies. Instead, it was a slow, quiet release that snuck up on me, catching me off-guard. One moment I was floating in a kaleidoscopic current of colors and feelings, and the next, tears were rolling down my cheeks without any clear reason. I felt embarrassed at first, unsure if I was losing control or doing something wrong. But as the session folded into itself, I recognized those tears as part of a deeper process - a kind of emotional melting that felt like healing.

Over the years, I’ve witnessed countless helpers, therapists, and fellow travelers grapple with these moments. Sometimes tears arise from grief, sometimes from release, sometimes from joy. Each wet thread carries its own story, but the neuroscience behind these reactions opens a window into why crying can be one of the most healing parts of a session. It’s not just about emotion - it’s about how the brain and body process trauma, attachment, and growth.

In this article, I want to unravel the mystery behind crying during psychedelic sessions, exploring the science and research that explain why tears heal. I’ll share practical insights on how to approach these moments, common challenges that emerge, and how to integrate this experience into everyday life. If you’ve ever felt vulnerable or confused by tears on a trip, this is for you.

2. Core Concept Explanation with Scientific Backing

At its core, crying is a biological response linked tightly to our emotional and nervous systems. It’s not just an overflow of sadness or happiness; it’s a neurochemical and physiological event with deep evolutionary roots. When we cry during a psychedelic session, there’s often a convergence of several brain systems reacting simultaneously.

Scientists have identified tears as part of the parasympathetic nervous system’s activation, specifically linked to the vagus nerve, which regulates calm and connection. When this system kicks in, it helps the body downshift from fight-or-flight stress into a state of surrender and restoration. Psychedelic substances like psilocybin or LSD disrupt the default mode network (DMN), a brain system responsible for our rigid sense of self and habitual thought patterns. When the DMN quiets, emotional material once locked away can surface without the usual filters of judgment or resistance.

This disruption, as studied by Robin Carhart-Harris and his team at Imperial College London, fosters what they call “ego dissolution,” where the boundaries between self and other soften. As parts of the brain that usually censor or suppress painful memories loosen their grip, repressed material - often trauma, grief, or unresolved feelings - can erupt into consciousness. The crying we see is a natural expression of this release, a way the brain and body communicate and process complex emotions.

Neurochemically, crying involves oxytocin and endorphins, sometimes referred to as “feel-good” chemicals. Oxytocin promotes social bonding and reduces fear responses, which can explain why crying often brings a sense of relief or connection after the initial flood of emotion. Endorphins act as natural painkillers, which may be why crying feels cathartic rather than purely painful.

What’s fascinating is that psychedelics also increase neuroplasticity - the brain’s ability to form new connections. This means that crying during a session isn’t just an emotional vent; it’s a moment where the brain is rewiring itself, making room for healthier patterns of thinking and feeling. That’s why tears during these sessions aren’t just “going through the motions” - they’re part of a deep healing process happening at the neural level.

3. Research and Evidence - Citing Studies

Scientific interest in the emotional aspects of psychedelic therapy has blossomed over the past two decades, and crying has emerged as a notable phenomenon. One of the most influential studies comes from Roland Griffiths at Johns Hopkins University. In his pioneering research on psilocybin, Griffiths documented not only mystical experiences but also intense emotional releases, including crying, which participants often described as life-changing and healing.

Another significant contribution comes from Carhart-Harris and his team, who have mapped the brain activity of people on psychedelics using fMRI scans. Their 2016 study showed that psychedelics reduce activity in the DMN while increasing connectivity between areas that don't usually talk to each other. This rewiring is linked to emotional breakthroughs, including crying, as suppressed feelings become accessible.

Beyond neuroimaging, trauma researcher Bessel van der Kolk’s work helps us understand why crying is a healing force. In his book The Body Keeps the Score, van der Kolk highlights how trauma is stored in the body and how somatic release - like crying - can open this trapped pain. Psychedelic therapy often touches these deep trauma layers, allowing for embodied emotional release. When tears flow during a session, it’s frequently the body communicating what words can't express.

Stanislav Grof, one of the pioneers of psychedelic therapy, emphasized the therapeutic value of “emotional discharge” in altered states. He observed that tears, shaking, or laughing often accompany the release of blocked trauma and are signs of “holotropic healing.” Grof’s work suggests that crying is not a side effect but a central part of the healing journey.

Lastly, microdosing entrepreneur and mycologist Paul Stamets has pointed out how substances like psilocybin mushrooms may support emotional health indirectly by promoting neurogenesis and reducing inflammation. His Host Defense Lion's Mane Capsules advocate, for instance, is rooted in supporting brain function and emotional resilience. While microdosing is different from full psychedelic sessions, both show how fungi affect brain chemistry in ways that support emotional expression, including crying.

4. Practical Application: Bringing Crying into Your Psychedelic Work

If you’ve read this far, you’re probably wondering how to work with your tears in a psychedelic session or even prepare for them. The first thing I want you to know is that crying is not a failure or a sign that you’re “too emotional.” It’s a signal that your brain and body are doing their job - processing deep feelings. The more you accept and allow this, the more healing you invite.

Start by setting an intention for your session that includes openness to whatever emotions arise, including tears. Don't try to control or suppress your crying. Instead, cultivate a witnessing attitude, like a calm friend sitting beside you, gently holding space. This allows the emotions to flow without judgment or interference.

One tip I’ve seen help many people is to have tissues or a soft cloth within reach. That physical act of wiping tears can feel grounding. Some therapists encourage participants to speak about their tears if they feel called, while others suggest silent acceptance. Both approaches are valid. Just remember that crying is a form of communication, even if no words come.

After the session, consider journaling about your tears. Sometimes writing helps to understand what the emotion was about and how it connects to your life story. If you feel stuck, reading a book like How to Change Your Mind by Michael Pollan can provide context and validation for your experience, reminding you that these emotional releases are part of a larger process.

Another practical step is to complement your sessions with supportive supplements that promote brain health and emotional balance. For example, Host Defense Lion's Mane Capsules are renowned for supporting neurogenesis and cognitive clarity, which can make emotional processing smoother over time.

Finally, if crying brings up difficult memories or you feel overwhelmed, having a trusted sitter, therapist, or integration coach can make all the difference. They can help you move through emotional terrain and offer grounding techniques to stay present with your feelings, rather than running from them.

5. Common Challenges and How to Work Through Them

Crying in a psychedelic session can be deeply healing, but it’s not always easy to sit with. Many people fear losing control or worry that the tears mean something is “wrong.” These anxieties can create resistance, which paradoxically makes it harder for the healing tears to flow.

One common challenge is feeling exposed or vulnerable. Tears strip away defenses and can feel like peeling back layers you’re not ready to show. In these moments, remind yourself that vulnerability is strength, not weakness. Your brain is rewiring, and tears are part of the process. If sitting with vulnerability feels impossible, try grounding techniques such as slow breathing, feeling your feet on the ground, or gently squeezing a soft object.

Another challenge is confusion about what the tears mean. Sometimes tears come without any clear trigger, which can be unsettling. In sessions where the mind is quieted or altered, emotional releases may not have immediate explanations. Allow yourself to accept the mystery. Later, through journaling or therapy, insights will often surface.

Some people struggle with shame around crying. Cultural conditioning tells many of us that crying is “unmanly” or “weak,” especially men. Recognizing these internalized messages and questioning them can be freeing. If shame arises, try self-compassion statements like, “This is natural. I am safe. It's okay to feel this way.”

Physical discomfort can also disrupt emotional release. Tears sometimes bring a tight throat or headache, which can trigger panic or withdrawal. If this happens, pause and remind yourself it’s temporary and part of the body’s release. Drinking water, gentle stretching, or asking for support can help.

Finally, some people fear that crying will spiral into a breakdown. In reality, crying in psychedelic sessions is usually self-limiting. The brain’s release mechanisms tend to restore balance after a period of emotional surge. However, if you feel you need extra support, don’t hesitate to speak with a mental health professional who understands psychedelic experiences.

6. Integration with Daily Life - Long-Term Perspective

Crying during a session is a moment, but its ripple effects can last for months or years. The challenge is figuring out how to carry these tears and what they mean back into everyday life. Integration is where the real healing solidifies, and crying is a key bridge in this process.

When you bring the memory of crying into your daily life, you remind yourself that emotions can be experienced fully and safely. This often shifts your relationship with vulnerability and emotional expression outside of sessions. For example, you may find yourself more willing to cry during movies or difficult conversations, signaling a growing emotional fluidity.

Part of integration is paying attention to what the tears were releasing. Did they open a door to grief you hadn’t allowed? Did they reveal a pattern of repression or self-judgment? Working with a therapist or journaling can help map out these emotional discoveries, so you stop carrying old burdens unknowingly.

Physical practices like yoga, breathwork, or somatic therapy can also support ongoing emotional release and prevent bottling up feelings after a psychedelic journey. Remember, the body keeps score, and crying is one way the body communicates, but movement and touch help too.

Supplements like Host Defense Lion's Mane Capsules may also support this integration by enhancing neuroplasticity, helping your brain retain new pathways formed during psychedelics. This physical support can make emotional patterns easier to shift over time.

Lastly, reading books like The Body Keeps the Score or How to Change Your Mind anchors your experience in a larger context of healing and neuroscience. This makes integration feel less lonely and more grounded in knowledge.

7. Closing Reflection: A New Thought to Sit With

Here’s something I’ve come to believe deeply: crying during a psychedelic session is a form of listening - listening to parts of yourself you usually ignore or silence. It’s the brain’s way of opening a conversation between your inner world and your body, between past pain and present healing. In those tears, there’s a message, a language beyond words.

What if instead of fearing or dismissing tears, we imagined them as sacred messengers? What if every drop carried a tiny spark of transformation? The next time you cry on a journey, don’t rush to understand or fix it. Sit with the sensation, like you’d sit with a quiet friend. Let the tears teach you patience, humility, and presence.

In a world that often prizes strength as silence and control, crying reminds us that true strength is sometimes the courage to surrender. Psychedelics don’t just show you visions or dissolve your ego. They invite you to meet your own humanity in the rawest, most tender way. Those tears aren’t a sign of weakness; they’re proof that you’re alive, feeling, and moving toward wholeness.

So, the next time you find yourself wiping away tears in a psychedelic session or even in everyday life, consider this: you are not alone. Your brain, your body, and your heart are working together to help you heal - and sometimes, healing is simply letting yourself cry.
